segments.text: Hello,
good morning, is it still? Yes.
Yes.
Wonderful.
Just.
We've been here a while.
Welcome. In the interest of time, while everyone's getting themselves seated,
we're in public session for the bill on the
Health and Safety at Work Act.
Can we just ask that you have
10 minutes, and that includes time for questions, and take
the
report or your submission as read, and highlight what you like, and the time is
over to you.
Understood.
I'm ready to go.
Yes, please.
Great. Okay. Good morning. Thank you, Madam Chair.
Thank members of the committee for the opportunity to appear for Civil Contractors
New Zealand regarding Health and Safety at Work Amendment Bill. I'm Fraser May.
I'm the communications and advocacy manager at CCNZ.
We're the National Association for Horizontal Construction.
And with me here today, I've got Managing Director of Pipeline and Civil, Mr.
Hugh Goddard.
Hi.
He's a CCNZ member. His business is primarily involved in construction and
maintenance of water networks, but he's also a board member of Construction Health
and Safety New Zealand and an independent director of roading company
Informax. So yeah, pretty good board experience.
CCNZ, we've got more than 800 member businesses and organizations responsible
for constructing and maintaining roads, water, energy, and more.
Our sector operates in high-risk environments,
underground tunnels, trenches, large machinery, live traffic
lanes, soil stabilization halfway up a cliff.
You name it, the members are out there doing it.
Our comments are pretty simple, really.
Reform should deliver genuine safety improvements, provide clarity
for duty holders, and be practical and workable across infrastructure
delivery at all scales.
We will have the submission as read.
I just wanted to focus on a couple of key points.
The cost of industry-- injuries, sorry, to business is very real and
has been projected in the billions annually.
But that's to the economy as well, and most of all to the workers who
experience the injuries. So I think, we've all got a stake in stopping that from
happening. We support the bill's objectives of clarifying and
streamlining health and safety obligations.
We do welcome emphasis on prioritizing critical risk,
but we feel that the proposals that have been put forward do not adequately capture
the risk, and we feel some changes are needed.
So risks, we do not feel, are dependent on business size.
So, the 20 work definition of a small PCBU
is not workable. We feel it's arbitrary, and it's not going to really
be applicable to construction settings.
Those businesses I talk to working halfway up a cliff, they are often
10 workers maybe. But they operate in some of the highest risk settings out
there, and they need to manage that.
We appreciate clear guidance on governance responsibilities, but we think a bit
more is needed, especially where an officer
holds a governance and an operational role within a business.
We need clarity on that. We need fair regulatory
responsibilities, and there needs to be strong accountability for upstream
clients. We can't put people at risk of injury to cut corners or cut
costs. Clients pay for the project, and they set the table for
success or failure. So having them say, "We don't play the health and safety game
with me," could put people at risk, and we can't have that happening.
Everyone's got a role to play. What we're asking for is robust, industry-connected
regulatory guidance and active consultation with our member
businesses to ensure the changes can be implemented effectively and
supported by practical codes of practice.
We'll leave time for questions, but I'm going to hand over to Hugh now for his
perspective and a couple of examples from the contractors' perspective.
Yeah. Thanks very much, Fraser. And hi, everyone.
So I guess, giving an example around the proposed PCBU
carve-out. So in our sector, risk doesn't change depending on whether the person
standing beside the trench or a live service or a piece of plant is employed by a
business with 15 workers or with 50. Risk is the same.
So the consequences if things go wrong are the same, so I think that's a key thing.
On a typical civil project, we may have a main contractor, specialist
subcontractors, labor-only teams, surveyors, traffic management, plant
operators, reinstatement teams, all working together.
And sometimes this equates to hundreds of people from different companies on-site
at any one time. So I guess the challenge is for a WorkSafe person going
into that site, how do they know if they're dealing with a PCBU that actually has
less than or greater than 20 workers?
And if the law applies differently depending on the size, it doesn't simplify site
management. In practice, the head contractor controlling the PCBU will still need
one common
standard across the site.
Mm-hmm.
So that's just going to create, instead of less bureaucracy,
it's going to create more administration, more headaches, I guess, and more
challenges and confusion.
I think we've covered off that small businesses doesn't equate to smaller risks.
So I think that point was well made by Fraser.
And in critical risk, we certainly support direction
in principle, and I think the more important issue from a contractor's perspective
is defining
and making sure the critical controls are identified, understood, resourced,
monitored. That's where the rubber hits the road in terms of controls rather than
just identifying the risks, and that's what prevents catastrophic events from
occurring.
Obviously, in New Zealand, we don't have the kind of health and safety record that
justifies lowering the bar,
and we should be looking to raise standards,
not dilute them. The cost to harm is significant.
It's borne by the sector itself through disruption, reduced productivity, lost
capability, and poor outcomes for workers and families, but it's also borne more
widely through ACC and the public health system.
Mm-hmm.
So, I think there's a big challenge here.
And in my view, the pathway to a safer, more productive construction sector is
not to water down safety laws, to have a strong and well
resourced regulator, clear and practical guidance around what good looks like,
and an industry where expectations are consistent and
understood.And that will help create a future where work is done by
people who are safe, healthy, well-trained, properly qualified, more engaged,
and ultimately, more productive.
So the practical message really is the biggest opportunity here isn't to increase
complexity by creating a two-tier system of duties.
Let's create a system that is clear, more practical, and more useful for the people
actually delivering work on the ground.
So, our original submission to the MB consultation and subsequent discussions with
the minister call for clear guidance through ACOPS-
Mm
... and similar tools, and that remains unchanged. And thanks very much.
And looking forward to questions.
Thank you. Just a couple of quick questions from myself.
We've heard a lot about the carve-out for small business, but we have,
and not to just throw it out, we have heard from some that say that that is
incredibly helpful, like in farming, for example.
Do you see a workable way where you could carve out industry rather
than size?
So, for example, we've heard from transport and from yourselves that you don't
see a differentiation between small and large for risk.
But yet there might be
small offices, like an accounting firm, where that small PCBU works.
Mm.
So do you see that working from your perspective?
I might set off on that one if I could here.
Yeah.
I think it does depend on the level of risk.
So absolutely, that might make sense for a dairy owner, to have less paperwork or
less unclear obligations. But what risks are they
facing, is the critical thing here.
So, if we're working in the top 10% of risk the whole time,
and pretty much every civil construction site out there will be-
Mm
... we need to manage that well. Whether that's, and how that's handled through the
legislation matters.
Mm.
I think, a blanket cutoff will be very difficult for us to
manage because it creates a double standard for people that are
working, and it's not really applicable.
Yeah. I'd also just add, I guess, around what we call risk amplifiers, so things
like fatigue, psychosocial harm-
Mm
... stress, all these sorts of factors are risk amplifiers.
So although you might be working around an excavator-
Mm
... that could kill you if something went wrong.
If that person's tired, subject to bullying, harassment, or whatever-
Mm
or fatigue or other sort of factors, that's going to
obviously influence the outcome.
And I think, having less focus on those risks and obviously musculoskeletal
is a big area as well.
There's a significant challenge in the construction sector.
And by de-emphasizing the importance of businesses to manage those, you're creating
some challenges.
Yeah. Phil.
Just, I want to make sure I understand your main point, and thank you
for a very compelling submission.
But you seem to be, your argument, I think, if I hear you right, is
that the level of risk is unrelated to
the size-
Yeah
... of the enterprise or the number of employees. Is that right?
Correct.
Correct.
So the PCBU cutoff, the
two-tier system that this bill creates-
Mm
doesn't pertain to actual risk. Is that right?
Correct.
Yeah.
So that's what I'm saying about having it carved out, industry rather than
size.
Yeah. I guess,
in your submission you say the committee should ensure the bill does not create a
legislative signal that these risks, that is the psychosocial risks outside
the critical risk definition, that these risks are secondary or optional,
but doesn't it, by definition, it's central to the concept of the whole
bill, that those risks are being deprioritized, aren't
they?
Well, I suppose you just take mental health as an example.
I'm not sure if I fully,
maybe you come back to your question-
Yeah
... to make sure I'm clear on it. But
we're losing a person a week to suicide in
construction.
Right.
So as a result of lots of factors.
Yep.
But obviously by de-emphasizing that for smaller businesses,
which actually the majority of, I know 90 plus percent of
construction businesses in New Zealand are small businesses by definition, and
probably fall-
Yeah
... largely within the criteria of less than 20.
We're
not addressing a massive issue that we've got and I think that's where the
challenge is that we need to
tackle.
Thank you. Question from Vanessa Wenick.
So in your submission, you highlight catastrophic risk.
Mm.
Can I just have an example of what you might mean by
catastrophic risk?
Explosion of a bitumen tanker.
Mm.
It's a very low likelihood of something like that happening.
But obviously the consequences are significant.
So by, I guess, how we capture and utilize the
word likelihood-
Okay
... in this case, needs to be expanded-
Yeah
... in the bills further.
Can I just-- So when you're doing a
risk analysis matrix, basically-
Yeah
... the likelihood and the outcomes are the kind of
thing. To me,
the
outcomes are the only things that have been focused on potentially in looking at
this and that schedule of whether it's death or injury
to eyes or those kind of things.
Whereas actually,
and then the likelihood of that happening creates a critical risk
rather than a catastrophic risk, which is probably
never happen. But if it does-
Mm
... holy crap, you know, sort of thing. Yes.
Yeah. That's it.
Yeah.
Yeah.
If we lose a fuel line to the Interislander or something, that's like-
Yeah.
So just one thing I wanted to raise was one thing that we do support, and we
probably didn't talk about this, and that is the role of WorkSafe and the
prioritization.
Mm-hmm.
I think
the problem we've got at the moment is that it's not entirely clear what people's
responsibilities are.
Industry has guidance like bitumen burns cards or-
Mm
... bitumen safety handbooks.
Mm.
But getting WorkSafe to endorse those and say, "This is a reasonable
standard-
Yeah
... this is reasonably practicable," is
very, very difficult at the moment-
Correct. Mm
... to this stage. And that is what we really need.
Yeah.
And I think-
That's the role for them
... the way that the legislation has looked at that is
perhaps more spot on as long as WorkSafe's properly resourced and encouraged
to do that job, that that would support that.
Thank you. That is all the time we've got with you, but we found your submission
very helpful and-
Yeah. Thank you guys
... thank you for your time.
Thanks so much.
Thank you very much, Mia.
Thank you.
Wonderful.
Yes.
Perfect. We have now got Duane Leo, Tracey
Klenner, and Paula Warren from the Health Sector Committee of the
PSA.
Welcome. We have 10 minutes with you this morning, and that includes time for
questions, and the time is yours now. Over to you.
Tēnā koe chair and members of the committee.
Thank you for the chance for us to speak today on behalf of our members and the
union. My name is Duane Leo. I'm one of the national secretaries for the New
Zealand Public Service Association, and to my left, I have Tracey Klenner, who
is our health and safety lead, and I also have
Paula Warren, who's our PSA National Delegates Committee and also one of our
national delegates at DOC.
So you've received three submissions from different parts of the
PSA.
One overall submission, one from the members of our health and safety sector
committee, and one from the members of the PSA National Delegates Committee at
Department of Conservation. We have over 96,000 members working in all
parts of public and community services around the country.
That's a wide range of experiences and perspectives.
Our health and safety sector committee and the DOC National Delegates Committee
wanted to be here to share their perspectives on the bill with you, and we're
disappointed you don't have the opportunity to hear from them.
So in terms of the proposal, we have summary point one.
The PSA submits that the bill, as drafted, risks weakening worker
protections by narrowly focusing on critical risks and carving out
small PCBUs for core duties.
Mm.
So in terms of that, the bill's emphasis is narrow, and the definition of critical
risk could exclude high-frequency, everyday hazards,
multiskeletal injuries, slips, trips, falls,
workplace violence, and incidents that drive most ACC
costs and harm to workers.
Because of the poorly worded definition of critical risk, all PCBUs will
no longer have to prioritize risks that fall outside the definition
but can have quite significant health impacts.
A clear example of this would be where workers are exposed to a combination of
chemicals or hazardous substances that together cause
long-term health risks, for example, the likes of cancer.
These types of exposures are not captured by Schedule 2,
but are very common in instances with firefighters, lab
workers, wood processing, manufacturing.
All PCBUs would no longer have to prioritize managing these risks,
directly resulting in a less safe workplace.
Using the ACC occupational disease schedule to define critical health
risks is inappropriate and risks leaving important work-related
illnesses outside the scope of duty.
Thanks, Duane. Kia ora. I'm Tracey Klenner, as
Duane mentioned.
Our concern more broadly is with the problem with the small PCBU
carve-out. So, we believe this is exempting or
weakening duties for small PCBUs employing 20 or under
workers will create a two-tier health and safety system.
Fragmented accountability in shared workplaces and likely increase injuries.
All workers have the right to go home from work safe.
It shouldn't matter how small their workplace is.
It'll be workers and their families and ACC that will bear the cost of
these unmanaged risks. And as the union for people working in health and safety
regulation, we also submit that in workplaces where PCBUs have to
deal with both large and small organizations, this will create confusion and
enforcement difficulties if different standards apply.
So, if we look at this from the point of view of my workplace, Department of
Conservation,
most of our staff are working with people who are from small businesses.
So they're dealing with rural contractors, they're dealing with the
people who build huts,
who fix our cars, and they're going into other people's workplaces.
And what that means is that we will have to put a lot more cost and
effort into managing the risks that are faced by
our people working with those businesses.
Mm.
So there's not going to be an overall reduction in cost.
There's just going to be a higher confusion.
So when I have raised this issue with a health and safety manager, they have said,
"Well, everybody who comes into our workplace will have to comply with our laws."
That means that we will have to re-look at every contract,
including for concessionaires-
Mm
... who work on our lands. So, it's just going to be
very difficult to manage.
In addition, we have concerns about the approved codes of practice and the
regulator role. Approved codes of practice
must be developed with WorkSafe, with the participation of unions and workers,
be regularly reviewed and act as guidance without setting a
ceiling on reasonably practicable duties.
We're narrowing WorkSafe's oversight to only catastrophic risks,
risks enforcement gaps, and inconsistent sector
outcomes. We also have concerns about the problem with relying on
other enactments, so proposed Section 35 treats compliance with other
regulatory regimes
as equivalent to Health and Safety at Work Act duties.
It's unsafe. Many laws, wildlife, marine, education,
earthquake compliance, corrections regulations, are designed for other
purposes and do not protect worker safety.
This should not proceed.
So in terms of the economic and system consequences, so if these changes are
made and everyday hazards are deprioritized, ACC claims,
health system demand, and productivity losses, they will rise, shifting
costs on to the
state, onto workers, and onto the workers' families and
communities. We all want a country where we're all confident that our children and
our family members and our friends will come home safe from work.
The bill makes this less likely, and we do not support it.
In terms of our ask, what we're asking from the committee,
we're asked to recommend that the bill does not progress in its current form.
Please require amendments to retain universal health and safety duties
for all PCBUs. Remove the small PCBU carve-out
to keep all workers safe. Broaden the scope beyond the critical
risks.Tighten Section 35 and mandate WorkSafe
worker and union participation in the approved codes of
practice. And thank you, that's our presentation for this morning.
Thank you very much. We have three questions.
Hopefully, we can get through all of them. We have the first question from Carl.
In the industries and the work sites that the PSA covers,
is there any correlation between
level of risk
and size of enterprise, number of employees in a PCBU?
Sue.
Yeah, I think
there is some clear research, but really hard to come by,
that there are a number of
more risks in smaller places, and
because larger places, as corrections, for example, where
they
do have some very high risks-
Yeah
... but they have really robust health and safety systems.
So there's higher risk-
That includes workers-
... in smaller workplaces.
Because there is often less of an internal system in the background,
right?
Right. Yeah.
Corrections have got a multitude of health and safety governance committees, and
workers are involved right from the floor up.
Similarly, at the Department of Conservation, another place with quite high level
critical risks.
Yep.
People working under helicopters and in helicopters.
And yet this bill weakens the protections for those smaller workplaces.
The most vulnerable.
Yep.
Yeah.
Thank you.
Yeah. And so again, generally outside the public
service, the businesses that we work with that have the highest
risks-
Yep
... helicopters, small boats,
construction at height, abseiling for weed control,
the use of toxic sprays, these are all small businesses-
Mm
... that we use for those.
Thank you. If we can try and get to Carl Bates and then Ricardo,
and then Desmond.
Thanks, Chair. Thanks for your submission.
In your experience, in all of the involvement you've had in health and
safety,
one of the
aims of this bill is to reduce the compliance burden for business,
in the health and safety management of risk.
Where have your members or your own engagement,
any recent examples of recommending changes
to health and safety practice that have removed
unnecessary compliance burdens in your
workplaces, or is it always layering on
another layer of requirements, activity, compliance?
I think most of the workplaces where our members were,
it is the simple process of including the worker voice in any conversation that's
held around health and safety. It doesn't create lots of extra time or
complexity, and it works well, and conversations occur.
Even in a small, like if we look at Ministry for Women, for example, it's a small
place. They don't have a huge health and safety system.
They work alongside the government health and ...
by the Public Service Commission.
And, so it doesn't create lots of extra paperwork and lots of extra time because
they share that among one another.
So, does, is that answer to your question?
So my question is-
Yeah
... are there any examples of where you've gone, "That process actually isn't
needed. We're taking it away," that you can think of?
No, I don't think ... I think the issue here from our perspective is
where there's good employers and when they're working
together with their staff, with the unions, there's not seen to be
that much detail and complexity. It's embedded in business as usual.
They don't see it as an over and above and extra thing they have to do, and that's
with good employers, and that's the rationale there.
And in our submission, we do note the surveys that we ran, both
with our wider health and safety advocates, but also with members
within, for example, WorkSafe. Where there's, again, the perception
that there's that complexity just isn't there where you've got a good functioning
health and safety workplace, and they're constantly looking to renew, refresh,
and update. So I don't necessarily think you'd call that additional layers of
bureaucracy. It's just they're constantly making sure that they're addressing the
need in the workplace.
Thank you.
Yeah.
Thank you. That's all the time we have with you.
We appreciate your-
Thank you
... very detailed submissions and your participation in the process.
Lovely, Chair. Thank you, Committee.
All right. We now have the Tertiary Education Union joining us online.
And we have Megan Morris and Zoe Port.
Welcome.
Zoe, have we got Megan separately, or is she going to be in the same room with
you?
No. Kia ora, sorry. Megan, I've been told, is also in the Zoom.
She's in a different location. It might be that she's just changing. Here she is.
Oh, here we are. Wonderful.
Hi.
Okay. We have 10 minutes with you, that includes time for questions. Over to you.
I'm starting, eh, Zoe?
Tena koutou. Ko Megan Morris toku ingoa.
Ko Te Pō Matauranga me Te Pō Ahumahi tohu tuaharange
Aotearoa. I hold a national role as our union's
education industrial campaigns officer, and my experience includes about 15
years as a health and safety trainer for the CTU WorkSafe Rep program,
along with policy development and consultation.
I did a lot during COVID, the pandemic, and I've worked
across health, in aged care in my previous
life before I joined the tertiary sector, and I've been in the tertiary sector for
about the last 20 years.
More recently, though, we've been working increasingly in the,
or I have, in the psychosocial hazard and risk area around developing
courses, providing advice and technical support internally and
externally with other unions.
As our submission states, we work across the entirety of the tertiary sector.
We have over 11,000 members, and
we strongly oppose the proposed changes to this
billThe stated objectives of the proposed amendment
bill are to reduce unnecessary compliance costs, increase certainty
for business, and finally, to support continued reductions in the
incidents of workplace fatalities, injuries, and illnesses.
And it says a lot to
those of us who work predominantly with workers, that the priorities of
the proposed amendments seem to be that
compliance and businesses first, and worker health and safety is the
last objective.
One of the other stated areas of concerns around leading to the
amendments was around the confusion and over-compliance
from many PCBUs in the consultation period,
which to be clear from a worker perspective and the unions,
was overwhelmingly excluded worker voice through that process, all of the
meetings held around the country.
So we don't genuinely feel that this is a
good
representation of any necessary amendments to the
current legislation.
The focus on critical risk is really concerning for us.
It really exposes the gendered nature of the proposed changes, who
ignore women who make up largely about approximately 50% of the
workforce. And for all of those who work in female-dominated
occupational areas like health and education.
The biggest issues that we deal with are in the sort of hazards and
risk around in the psychosocial space.
So things like workload, continuous restructuring in our sector is a
massive problem, and that creates huge
issues around stress and fatigue, et
cetera. Also,
the
amendments largely ignore the musculoskeletal issues like
slips, trips, and falls.
And again,
harassment, bullying,
violence in the workplace.
The psychosocial area is often seen as invisible,
and the impacts of
being
affected by those sorts of things is really
wide-ranging and can often, will generally be
taken home to family, friends, et cetera.
If we look outside of our sector, for example, the focus on critical risk for
small businesses could potentially push the cost of PPE
onto workers. For example, workers in the care and support sector
who already been denied pay equity settlements, they travel massive
distances,
and they use their own vehicles with a completely inadequate petrol
reimbursement. But they're one of the most vulnerable groups in our workforce.
And if you are asking
them,
well, the bill could potentially ask them to provide their own PPE, for example.
So they already earn low wages, they cover their own petrol, they use their own
cars, and now they're having to provide their own PPE because the type of work that
they do would not fit the definition of critical risk that has been put into
this bill.
Or if you look into sort of trades and manufacturing and spaces like that,
most of the PPE available for workers is actually
designed by men for men. So it doesn't fit women very well, and if you
happen to be a petite woman, you've got serious problems trying to find
PPE that actually fits. And the point of the PPE
is that it is meant to provide appropriate
protection, whether it's overalls or respirators or helmets,
whatever it might be, steel cap boots, et cetera.
It's meant to provide you with that element of protection so that when you do your
job, you can do it safely.
That would not occur.
The amendment bill will, in effect, create a two-tier system of
managing health and safety, which in our opinion, will only increase
confusion and uncertainty for PCBUs, not reduce
it. It will also
increase exposure for workers to hazards and risks,
especially in the small business space,
and do nothing to reduce the incidents of workplace fatalities,
serious injuries, or illnesses. So,
if we think about the point of the exercise with health and
safety legislation is about protecting workers and ensuring that
they can do their job safely and well, and go home to
their families safe and well, and not have ongoing
impacts from the job that they do affecting their everyday lives.
I don't believe that the changes in this legislation will actually
address that in any way, shape, or form.
I would also note that worker
voice is absolutely critical to ensuring good compliance with
legislation. So in our sector,
most of the employers are relatively large, and they do have reasonable
systems.
But the issues of
working with workers and their health and safety reps
across the
sector
is good in some places and not so good in others.
But we work really hard with those employers to ensure that they do have
robust systems in place that can protect our workers.
But it's not always perfect.
And we've got
in the psychosocial space, in particular, those are really hard to
deal with. And
it is critical for us that we ensure that there are
systems in place that can make that an easier process to manage.
And again, don't believe that the changes in this bill will do that.
I might just stop there and allow Zoe
Thanks so much, Megan. On that, I would like to just add, so Kia Ora,
Dr Zoe Port, lecturer in management at Massey University and TU
member. And my research expertise is particularly in the area of managing
psychosocial risks and creating healthy work. I teach and research in this area.
And I guess one thing I wanted to say is our sector, the tertiary
sector, it is meant to be grounded in evidence.
We must be evidence-informed in our teaching.
We don't just
convey whatever ideas we personally like to.
We base it on the research and the evidence, and I'd posit that many of the claims
upon which the most egregious changes in this bill are proposed, that
Megan has mentioned,
they are simply not grounded in evidence, and in many cases they
contradict evidence and expert opinion, including experts from the
past Pike River Royal Commission.
And just one point I want to drive home around psychosocial risk and hazards
as well, and why it is problematic that it essentially gets overlooked by the
narrow definition of critical risk, is that psychosocial hazards are
unique. They arise from the way work is designed, organized, and managed, things
like excessive workload, like Megan has said.
And the harm from them doesn't just stop when the worker
leaves the office at the end of the day or leaves the workplace.
Unlike a physical hazard that can only get me at work, psychosocial harm,
it can continue to manifest in the worker's mind, and the impact can continue
to weigh on them.
And it manifests not only in terms of mental health outcomes, but also physical.
For example, there's a huge body of international evidence on, say, psychosocial
hazards and cardiovascular disease.
So just to drive that home, we cannot be overlooking psychosocial hazards
as this definition does, because they cause just as much harm as more
visible hazards. I think being mindful of time, I will leave that there.
Thank you. We have run out of time with you, but do appreciate your participation
in the process. Thank you for being with us today.
Kia ora.
Kia ora.
We'll move on now to Retail New Zealand,
who are in the room,
and we'll come back to Bathurst Resources if they
join us.
Thank you very much. We've got Carolyn Young, Denise Garland, and Yevgenia.
Yevgenia.
Thank you. Chair.
Time is yours. Sorry, 10 minutes, includes time for questions.
Sorry. Thank you-
That's okay
... very much for the time. Really appreciate the opportunity to be here.
My name's Carolyn, as you mentioned, my colleague, Denise,
and Yev.
So look,
we know that you've read the submission, so we're not going to go through that.
Just want to highlight a couple of things, then we open for questions.
Look, in principle, we support the bill.
We think there's a couple of areas that need further clarification.
So our concerns are that this bill would
potentially give reason to believe that small retailers may not be

the drafting of the bill so critical risks are

The priority should be on
saying to people, "If they go to work, they will do everything we can to ensure you

By saying we're going to prioritize critical risk, basically says, "You

It's okay if you need some stitches because you've cut your

And
then to have someone come up and say, "Okay, now we're going to charge you because

it's the horse before the cart. You shouldn't put the horse before the cart.
We should be saying that everyone
needs to be safe,
yet we do have a priority, but the reality is there should be no difference.
The main priority should always be the focus, should be everyone who goes to

Lives matter.
And it's not just about whether you actually die, it's whether you have your

And that is also a concern.
Thank you very much, Corinna. Appreciate your participation in the

No worries.

Thanks for joining us. You've got 10 minutes with us.

Thank you, and appreciate the opportunity to speak.

Great.

Very briefly, and I won't repeat my submission, but my background

And my background is useful in a few ways.

I'm doing a PhD in risk at the University of Canterbury,

once implemented.
So that other sort of adjacent legislation piece,

work zones and any roadwork and construction has

20 plus or minus

organization sizes in the same contracting chain.
So a large contractor then has to figure out how those

That's a challenge for not just the lead contractor, but the client who's

The third and last point I'll make is around philosophy.
I see within this bill,
or we go back to the original philosophy of the health and safety system

It's centered around so far as reasonably practicable.
The idea that you are chasing
a
standard of continuous improvement, that if there's more you could do, you

In some cases, this bill inserts
a place where you can rest and say, "I've done enough."
The concept of ACOPS and
how they establish deemed compliance,
same in those adjacent legislation concepts.
And what that does is inserts
certainty where there is none in real life.
There is not certainty of that thing being effective.
And if you unwind that philosophy of the entire
act as it stands by inserting deemed compliance, then those two
things for me are incongruent. So there has to be a way to reconcile
that because it's not matching the philosophy of the overarching
act in its current form. And that's me.
Happy to chat through maybe if there's key areas of tension or if those ones are,

Can I just say impressive?
Because you've just gone straight to the point, and we've got lots of time for

Take the example of multiple contractors-

Isn't there an opportunity for the market to then say, "If you want to work on this

across a range of the trades?
Yep, sure. The market might decide that, and
so there's a pro and a con.
Yeah.
The
market decides who wins that work maybe, and how
they
put forward the commercial offering, and it's going to be based on whatever costs

But the client has to set the risk appetite to start with.

So I get that. I suppose the point I'm asking
is already in construction and in contracting,
there are additional requirements that are set by
contractors or by clients based on their circumstances, and the
same could occur
in relation to this point of small and large
PCBUs.
You're right. So a large contractor could establish a blanket expectation

organizations
who work with them in the contracting chain.
Correct.
If that small contractor has to then work in that higher

Not necessarily.
Not necessarily.
Because they already do it on other fronts.
At the moment, though, there isn't a two-tiered system.
Mm.
Correct.
Yeah.
But there are other requirements that contractors put on in relation

... in order to get the work.
Yep. That is obviously based on a risk assessment, because those

This is not addressing a risk, it's addressing a...
Well, this comes from a two-tiered system that generates a

Yeah.

Thank you very much.

Really helpful and
submission. We'll move on to Community Law

Mm-hmm. Kia ora. Kia ora koutou. My name is Rupert O'Brien.

our law centers generally see,
and represent, and advise employees at small,

risk in workplace doesn't align with the size of
an employer. It aligns with
the nature of the work undertaken.
And we also see that employees and small businesses already struggle

Well, generally there's no HR, there's no one to go to.

to the fore. And it's our experience that
when we tell people, "Well, look, your only other option other than talking

Our experience of WorkSafe as the regulator is that it

issues that are raised, especially with issues around
non-life-threatening risk, which is
the other issue that's been raised.
There's a serious risk.
Well, critical risk. There's serious issues with the critical risks

and the lack of certainty that
will be given rise by these changes.

and this critical risks
system that has been introduced doesn't do that.
We've got concerns that you might see some employers,

They may reclassify their own risks,
to be outside of the definition of critical risks.
It's very difficult to work out
what is meant in the bill by likely, what is a critical risk and how

And so whenever there's a gray area, we will see some

their requirements when it comes to them because there's a chance they can say,

And what we don't want to see is WorkSafe
having-To come back around after someone gets

I think the whole point that everyone would want to see is that

and especially work-related mental health concerns and
psychosocial harms.

"I've been injured. My doctor said they're not sure when I'm going to get back to

they're inviting me to a meeting to say they're going to medically terminate me."
And they will medically terminate them.
There will be no personal agreements or other way that we can get that person
any kind of compensation or improvement for their life, and
they will be then off, and whether they get ACC or not is not
a certainty, and they'll be then off work for a long time or forever.
And so
non-critical risks causing harm
are serious. They can cause serious problems for people's mental health as well as

It was the lack of proactive investigation that caused that, and the regulator

It's our opinion that the approach needed to protect life and limb and to

I'd also just quickly mention a concern that we've raised about the
possible industry ACOPS. While it's been submitted that ACOPS will be useful

we've repeatedly heard today that the regulator is not properly resourced, and

I'll just quickly go through that because of the nature of our work, we see

We see workers who have suffered injuries at work and are then dismissed for being

a difference to those people who work in small, non-unionized workplaces who will
be covered by that second tier of less obligation.
That's probably enough from me. I'll leave
time for questions if there are any.
Thank you very much, Rupert. It doesn't appear that we have any questions from the

You've clearly covered every point in your written and oral submission.
So thank you for being with us today, participating in the process.
Kia ora. Thank you.
Away from Phil. See ya.
We'll move on now to the Outdoor Access Commission.
We have Jeff Holgate online.
Jeff, if you could turn on your
camera and audio, we can
hear from you.
Good afternoon.
Good afternoon, Jeff.
Um-
We have 10 minutes with you, and that includes time for questions.
Thank you.
So it's all yours. Go for it.
Thank you.
Once again, good afternoon, Madam Chair and members of the committee.
My name is Jeff Holgate. I'm the regional field advisor, or a
regional field advisor with the Outdoor Access Commission here
in our Nuku Aotearoa,
and the presentation is on behalf of the commission.
You will have read that the commission is the Crown agent responsible for improving
and protecting outdoor public access.
Our governing piece of legislation is the Walking Access Act,
and the primary purpose of which is to protect and
enhance free, certain, and enduring practical access to the
outdoors.
Our submission is very succinct and limited in its
scope.
So I'd just like to start by referring to the Federated Farmers

where I heard David Burkett mention that farmers' confusion
about their responsibilities under the
Health and Safety Act,
produced...
Sorry, their confusion and fear of getting it

and that's very much our experience as well.
A number of cases where misunderstanding of land managers'

requirements has resulted in many, or quite a
lot of access being denied to the New Zealand
publicNow, apart from supporting some
aspects of the bill,
our written submission focuses on Clause 14, the proposed
changes to Section 37 of the act.
Even with those proposed amendments, we believe that in rural
settings, there may still be room for some misunderstanding.
Although the concept of a workplace is quite difficult to
grasp
in rural setting as opposed to an industrial setting.
So, in farming and forestry, for example, work can
occur virtually on all of the property at a number
of times.
So some PCBUs interpret that to mean
that the farm or forest is always a workplace, and
therefore,
they close it off to access because they believe they have

Even the definition of a worker has been questioned.
For example, it's been suggested that cows and calves grazing a

Therefore, the PCBU has responsibilities under Section

We strongly support further clarification that

includes recreational spaces
do not owe the
Section 37 duties to those lawfully accessing the

In most cases, we're talking about people simply walking across the
land
for recreation, or to get to a place of
recreation.
We don't believe the term open space in Section
14 (2) of the bill is appropriate.
Open space has got many different interpretations.
For example, would you consider a forest an open space?
I don't think many people instinctively would.
We've suggested other wording in our submission, which you will of course have

We believe additional clarity can
further be provided
in Section 14 (2) of the bill by including the

Now, it may seem like a bit of duplication, given the meaning of

But it would emphasize that for work to occur,

It's one of the difficulties we have in a lot of rural settings, is
people
don't realize that a worker has to be present to

Thank you.
